1: Then YHWH said to Moses, 'Go in to Pharaoh and say to him, Thus says YHWH, the God of the Hebrews, Let my people go, that they may serve me.' The fifth plague opens with the full formal title 'YHWH God of the Hebrews' (YHWH elohei ha-Ivrim) — the same identification used in the first formal confrontation (Exodus 3:18, Exodus 5:3, Exodus 7:16). The repeated formula after each rejection maintains the official character of the confrontation: this is a formal divine demand, not a negotiation.
2: 'For if you refuse to let them go and still hold them, behold, the hand of YHWH (yad YHWH) will fall on your livestock in the field, the horses, the donkeys, the camels, the herds, and the flocks — a very severe plague (dever).' Dever = pestilence/plague (a word used for epidemic disease throughout the OT; cf. Psalms 91:3,6). The livestock targeted include horses (susim) — Egypt's military technology — donkeys (chamorim), camels (g'malim), cattle (baqar), and flocks (tzon). The Egyptian economy was deeply dependent on livestock: cattle for agriculture, donkeys for transport, horses for military chariots. The plague's economic implications extended far beyond the immediate animal deaths.
3: 'Behold, the hand of YHWH will fall (hoyah yad YHWH) on your livestock in the field — a very severe plague.' The livestock plague targeted Egyptian animal cults directly. The Apis bull (Hapi = the sacred black-and-white bull kept at Memphis, associated with Ptah) was Egypt's most important sacred animal; its death would have been the most culturally shocking component of the plague. Hathor (cow goddess of love, beauty, and fertility) and Khnum (ram-headed creator god) were also challenged by a plague killing cattle and sheep.
4: 'But YHWH will make a distinction (hifla YHWH) between the livestock of Israel and the livestock of Egypt, so that nothing of all that belongs to the people of Israel shall die.' The Goshen distinction (established at the fly plague) is now explicitly applied to the livestock plague. The statistical impossibility of a cattle plague that kills Egyptian livestock but spares geographically adjacent Israelite livestock in Goshen is the evidential heart of the plague's theological claim: natural epidemics do not discriminate by covenant status.
5: And YHWH set a time, saying, 'Tomorrow YHWH will do this thing in the land.' The explicit advance announcement of timing (machar — tomorrow) escalates the theological precision: YHWH not only causes the plague but announces its precise start date. Pharaoh cannot claim ignorance of the source or coincidence of the timing. The time-announcement is a form of prophetic certitude: what YHWH declares will happen at the stated time.
6: And the next day YHWH did this thing. All the livestock of the Egyptians died, but not one of the livestock of the people of Israel died: The total Egyptian livestock death and the total Israelite survival are each emphasized with 'all' (kol) and 'not one' (lo echad). The absolute nature of both statistics rules out partial natural explanations. The livestock census of ancient Egypt (documented in administrative papyri) shows the scale of Egypt's cattle industry — the loss of all livestock would have been an economic catastrophe of the first magnitude.
7: And Pharaoh sent (vayyishlach), and behold, not one of the livestock of Israel was dead. But the heart of Pharaoh was hardened, and he did not let the people go: Pharaoh personally investigates the report of Israelite livestock survival — and confirms it. The investigation (vayyishlach = he sent, presumably investigators to check) and the verification (hinneh lo-met mimikneh Yisrael ad-echad = not one of Israel's livestock was dead) makes the eighth hardening the most theologically stark. Pharaoh has personally verified the divine distinction; he has received empirical confirmation of YHWH's covenant-specific sovereignty. His continued refusal is not ignorance but defiance in the face of evidence.
8: And YHWH said to Moses and Aaron, 'Take handfuls of soot from the kiln (pich hakivshan), and let Moses throw it in the air in the sight of Pharaoh.' Pich = soot (from an Egyptian loanword connected to furnace carbon deposits); kivshan = kiln/furnace (the same word as the 'smoking furnace' of Genesis 15:17 in the Abrahamic covenant vision). The soot from an Egyptian kiln (possibly a lime-kiln or metal-smelting furnace — Egypt had extensive metallurgical operations near the Delta) thrown into the air is the ritual act that precipitates the boils. The use of soot from an Egyptian industrial furnace is symbolically appropriate: Egypt's industry produces its own plague.
9: 'And it shall become fine dust (avaq) over all the land of Egypt, and become boils (sh'chin) breaking out in sores on man and beast throughout all the land of Egypt.' Avaq = fine dust (the word for the dust kicked up by armies and whirlwinds in prophetic texts — Isaiah 5:24; Nahum 1:3); the soot becomes fine dust and covers Egypt. Sh'chin = boils/skin eruptions (the medical condition that afflicts Job in Exodus 2:7-8 and is listed among the Deuteronomic curses in Deuteronomy 28:27,35). The exact condition described (boils breaking into blisters) is consistent with anthrax, smallpox, or a secondary skin infection following livestock disease — the progression from cattle pestilence (plague 5) to human skin disease (plague 6) follows an epidemiologically plausible sequence.
10: So they took soot from the kiln and stood before Pharaoh. And Moses threw it in the air, and it became boils breaking out in sores on man and beast: The brevity of the execution narrative (three verbs: took, stood, threw) contrasts with the detailed instruction of Exodus 9:8-9, emphasizing the total compliance and immediate divine response. No Aaron involvement is mentioned here — the sixth plague is Moses's alone, perhaps foreshadowing the increasing directness of divine action as the plagues escalate.
11: And the magicians could not stand before Moses because of the boils, for the boils came upon the magicians and upon all the Egyptians: The chartumim make their final appearance in the plague narrative, physically defeated. The irony: the priests who tried to counter YHWH's plagues by replicating them are now unable to stand (yakhal la'amod lifnei Moshe) because they are afflicted by the very plagues they attempted to contest. Their incapacity is the decisive sign of the end of Egyptian magical counter-resistance. They will not appear again in the Exodus narrative.
12: But YHWH hardened the heart of Pharaoh, and he did not listen to them, as YHWH had spoken to Moses: The sixth plague's hardening is the first in the narrative attributed solely and explicitly to YHWH (without any mention of Pharaoh's own action). The earlier hardenings alternated between Pharaoh's self-hardening and YHWH's hardening; from here forward the divine agency in Pharaoh's hardening is emphasized. Paul cites this specific verse (Romans 9:17) to discuss divine sovereignty in election and hardening.
13: Then YHWH said to Moses, 'Rise up early in the morning and present yourself before Pharaoh and say to him, Thus says YHWH, the God of the Hebrews, Let my people go, that they may serve me.' The seventh plague warning begins with the most extended divine speech of the plague cycle (Exodus 9:13-19), including a theological explanation of why the plagues are occurring and why Pharaoh has not been destroyed already. This expansion in the middle of the plague cycle is the theological center of the entire narrative.
14: 'For this time I will send all my plagues (mageyfotai) on you yourself, and on your servants and your people, so that you may know that there is none like me in all the earth.' 'None like me in all the earth' (ein kamoni b'chol-ha'aretz) — the incomparability formula that will appear in the Song of the Sea (mi-kamokha ba'elim YHWH = who is like you among the gods, Exodus 15:11). The seventh plague introduces a new intensity: previous plagues targeted Egypt; now the plagues are aimed 'at your heart' (el-libecha). The heart of Pharaoh has been the battleground since the first hardening — now YHWH states explicitly that the purpose is to reach that heart.
15: 'For by now I could have put out my hand and struck you and your people with pestilence, and you would have been cut off from the earth.' The counterfactual — 'by now I could have destroyed you' — is a remarkable divine admission. YHWH is not at the limit of his power; he has been restraining himself. The reason Pharaoh is still alive is not Pharaoh's resilience but YHWH's purpose (Exodus 9:16). This verse is Paul's other citation from the Exodus plague narrative (Romans 9:17) alongside Exodus 9:12 — the two verses together form Paul's argument for divine sovereignty in mercy and hardening.
16: 'But for this purpose I have raised you up (he'emadticha), to show you my power, so that my name may be proclaimed in all the earth.' He'emadticha = I have caused you to stand/raised you up (Hiphil of amad); Paul cites this as 'for this purpose I raised you up' (eis touto se exegeira, Romans 9:17). The purpose of Pharaoh's existence — from YHWH's perspective — is to demonstrate divine power before the watching world. The proclamation of YHWH's name 'in all the earth' anticipates the nations' trembling at the news of the Exodus (Exodus 15:14-16) and Rahab's confession in Joshua 2:10-11.
17: 'You are still exalting yourself (mistalea) against my people and will not let them go.' Sala' = to exalt/lift oneself up (a rare Hithpael form, used for arrogant self-elevation); the language of Egyptian royal ideology inverted — Pharaoh who presents himself as the exalted one (the divine elevation of the pharaoh was expressed in his crown, his titles, his elevated throne) is charged with illegitimate self-exaltation against YHWH. The contrast between YHWH's exaltation (Exodus 15:1 — ga'oh ga'ah) and Pharaoh's arrogant self-elevation is the theological core of the plague narrative.
18: 'Behold, about this time tomorrow I will cause very heavy hail to fall, such as never has been in Egypt from the day it was founded until now.' Egypt's climate makes severe hailstorms very rare — the Nile Delta receives minimal rainfall and even less in the form of hail; genuine hailstorms are almost unknown in Upper Egypt. The hail plague is climatologically anomalous for Egypt in a way it would not be in Canaan or Mesopotamia. The 'never before' (asher lo-hayah kamohoo b'Mitzrayim) superlative places this plague outside the range of known Egyptian experience.
19: 'Now therefore send, get your livestock and all that you have in the field into safe shelter, for every man and beast that is in the field and is not brought home will die when the hail falls on them.' The advance warning with an opportunity for mitigation is unique among the plagues (except the Passover, where YHWH also provides specific protective instructions). The moral dimension: those Egyptians who took the warning seriously and protected their servants and livestock survived; those who ignored it did not. YHWH's judgment includes an escape clause for those who respond in faith, even within Egypt.
20: Then whoever feared the word of YHWH among the servants of Pharaoh hurried his slaves and his livestock into the houses: 'Whoever feared the word of YHWH among the servants of Pharaoh' — Egyptians who believed YHWH. This is the first mention of faith in YHWH among non-Israelites during the plague cycle. Their fear and response anticipates the 'mixed multitude' (erev rav) of 12:38 who left Egypt with Israel. YHWH's offer of protection extended to any Egyptian who took his word seriously — the plague was not inexorably universal for every individual in Egypt.
21: But whoever did not pay attention to the word of YHWH left his slaves and his livestock in the field: The two-way response to the divine warning establishes the plagues as a morally serious divine communication, not merely an impersonal natural disaster. Those who ignored the warning suffered the consequences of their own choice. The text is careful to distribute moral responsibility: some Egyptians responded in faith; some did not. The judgment falls on those who reject the warning, not on an undifferentiated 'Egypt.'
22: Then YHWH said to Moses, 'Stretch out your hand toward heaven, so that there may be hail in all the land of Egypt, on man and beast and every plant of the field, in the land of Egypt.' The gesture toward heaven (shamayim) is new — previous plague gestures were toward the water (Nile) or the earth (dust). The hail plague comes from above, from the sky (nut's domain in Egyptian cosmology). Nut (sky goddess), Shu (god of air), and Tefnut (goddess of moisture) are the Egyptian deities of the atmospheric realm now under YHWH's command.
23: Then Moses stretched out his staff toward heaven, and YHWH sent thunder and hail, and fire ran down to the earth. And YHWH rained hail upon the land of Egypt: Kol (thunder/voice of YHWH) + barad (hail) + esh (fire/lightning) — the tripartite storm. The combination of lightning and hail is described in Psalms 78:47-48 and Exodus 105:32-33 as the plague of hail. In ANE cosmology, the thunder-voice of a deity was the supreme expression of divine authority; Baal's 'voice' (thunder) was the central feature of Canaanite storm theology; YHWH's thunderstorm over Egypt is a direct assertion of sovereignty over the sky deities of the entire ancient Near East.
24: There was hail and fire flashing (esh mitlaqachat) continually in the midst of the hail, very heavy hail, such as had never been in all the land of Egypt since it became a nation: Esh mitlaqachat = fire taking hold within itself (a Hithpael participle of laqach = to take; the fire is described as self-perpetuating within the hail). This extraordinary combination — lightning fire enclosed within hailstones — is unprecedented in natural meteorology, marking the plague as miraculous rather than merely extreme-weather. The 'never before' claim (Exodus 38:24, cf. Exodus 9:18) establishes the plague as historically unique in Egyptian experience.
25: The hail struck down everything that was in the field in all the land of Egypt, both man and beast. And the hail struck down every plant of the field and broke every tree of the field: The agricultural devastation of the hail: every plant (esev) and every tree (etz) struck down. The destruction of trees and crops directly threatened Egypt's food supply; combined with the livestock losses from the preceding plagues, the cumulative economic damage to Egypt was catastrophic. Egyptian administrative texts describe the importance of tree crops (date palms, sycamore figs) to the Egyptian economy — their destruction was not trivial.
26: Only in the land of Goshen, where the people of Israel were, was there no hail: The Goshen exception continues — the geographical boundary of divine protection is maintained through the seventh plague. The hail plague is the fifth consecutive plague with explicit or implicit Goshen protection (flies, livestock, boils, hail); the spatial theology of divine protection is becoming unmistakable.
27: Then Pharaoh sent and called Moses and Aaron and said to them, 'This time I have sinned (chatati). YHWH is in the right (tzaddiq), and I and my people are in the wrong (ha-r'sha'im).' The most remarkable confession in the plague narrative: Pharaoh explicitly acknowledges his sin (chatati = I have sinned), YHWH's righteousness (tzaddiq), and his own and Egypt's wickedness (r'sha'im). This is the formal theological vocabulary of covenantal acknowledgment — the same language as the Psalmist's confession (Psalms 51:4: 'you are justified/tzaddiq in your words') and Daniel 9:14 ('YHWH our God is righteous/tzaddiq'). Pharaoh's confession is formally correct but, as Exodus 9:34-35 reveals, not genuinely penitent.
28: 'Plead with YHWH, for there has been enough of God's thunder and hail. I will let you go, and you shall stay no longer.' Pharaoh's promise is explicit: 'I will let you go' (ashallecha = I will send you out, the Piel of shalach). His previous offers were conditional ('if you go, not too far'); this is the most absolute promise of release yet. But it comes under the most extreme duress — the seventh plague has exceeded anything Egypt has experienced. The correlation between the severity of the plague and the completeness of Pharaoh's promise reveals that his compliance is entirely pain-driven, not theologically motivated.
29: Moses said to him, 'As soon as I have gone out of the city, I will stretch out my hands to YHWH. The thunder will cease, and there will be no more hail, so that you may know that the earth is YHWH's (la-YHWH ha'aretz).' 'The earth is YHWH's' — the theological conclusion Moses draws from the hail plague. This phrase (la-YHWH ha'aretz) is the basis of Psalms 24:1 ('the earth is YHWH's and the fullness thereof') and Paul's citation of it in 1 Corinthians 10:26. The hail plague, which controls the sky and the field, demonstrates that YHWH's sovereignty extends to all realms of the created order — not just the Nile (water), or the insects (earth's surface), but the heavens themselves.
30: 'But as for you and your servants, I know that you do not yet fear YHWH God.' Moses's prophetic discernment cuts through Pharaoh's confession: the fear of YHWH (yir'at YHWH) is not yet present. The confession of Exodus 9:27 was accurate words without corresponding reality. 'You do not yet (terem) fear' — the 'not yet' is both diagnostic and hopeful; it implies the possibility of genuine fear at some future point. The phrase 'yet/terem' appears in contexts of unrealized potential throughout the OT.
31: The flax (pishtah) and the barley (se'orah) were struck down, for the barley was in the ear and the flax was in bud: The agricultural chronology of the hail: barley (se'orah) ripening in the ear (aviv — the same word as the month name) and flax (pishtah) in bud (gibol = in bloom/bud). In Egypt, barley ripens in late February-early March; flax blooms at roughly the same time. This chronology places the seventh plague approximately one month before Passover (in the month of Aviv/Nisan = March-April), providing a rare internal calendar marker for the plague chronology. Barley and flax were two of Egypt's most economically important crops.
32: But the wheat and the emmer were not struck down, for they are late in coming up: Wheat (chitah) and emmer (kussemeth) ripen 3-4 weeks after barley in the Egyptian climate — late March to April. Their survival means Egypt's primary bread crops were spared the hail damage; they would be destroyed in the subsequent locust plague (Exodus 10). The detail is historically specific: the hail hit between harvest cycles, destroying the early crops (barley, flax) while leaving the main food crops still in the ground. This specificity is one of the features that has led scholars to take seriously the historical memory embedded in the plague narrative.
33: So Moses went out of the city from Pharaoh and stretched out his hands to YHWH, and the thunder and the hail ceased, and the rain no longer poured upon the earth: Moses prays outside the city — the prayer distance from Pharaoh and the city suggests a deliberate separation, the prophet of YHWH standing apart from the corrupt center of power. The rain (matar) cessation alongside the thunder and hail confirms that the 'hail' was part of a full storm system; Egypt genuinely received extraordinary precipitation during this plague.
34: But when Pharaoh saw that the rain and the hail and the thunder had ceased, he sinned again and hardened his heart, he and his servants: The return to the hardening-after-relief pattern — despite Pharaoh's explicit confession of sin (Exodus 9:27) and explicit promise of release (Exodus 9:28), the cessation of distress produces re-hardening. This pattern has been consistent since the frog plague (Exodus 8:15); Pharaoh's confession under duress is not conversion. The servants also harden — the institutional resistance of Egypt extends beyond the individual king to his entire court.
35: So the heart of Pharaoh was hardened, and he did not let the people of Israel go, just as YHWH had spoken through Moses: The closing formula 'as YHWH had spoken through Moses' (ka'asher dibber YHWH b'yad-Moshe) ties each plague-outcome back to the original divine prediction. YHWH's foreknowledge encompasses Pharaoh's repeated re-hardening; the trajectory of the plague cycle was known to YHWH from the beginning and communicated to Moses before it began. The plague narrative is not a divine improvisation but a planned demonstration of sovereign power proceeding according to divine purpose.
